```{r}
Figure_history

```


### Eroding resilience: recurrent heat stress drives repeat bleaching

Previous studies have indicated thatthermal tolerance may be increasing at rates as high as 0.1 °C per decade  (e.g. Lachs et al 2024), which would imply that high-frequency bleaching could be substantially mitigated under low-to-middle emissions scenarios (e.g. Bozec et al 2024). The aerial survey record offers a unique and direct test of this across the length of  the Great Barrier Reef.

Tracking individual reefs through their bleaching category at each event reveals increasing severity through time (Fig. 4a). The earliest events sit predominantly in the no-to-low categories, but occupancy of the higher categories grows steadily across the record, and the most severe "extreme" category appears only from 2022 onward. By the most recent events every repeatedly surveyed reef had been impacted by bleaching, and the ribbons tracing individual transitions show that reefs rarely return to an unbleached state once the reef-wide events of 2016 begin.

Decomposing these movements into transition probabilities, conditional on a reef's starting category, exposes an asymmetry that points to eroding recovery between events (Fig. 4b). Reefs beginning an interval in the low-to-moderate categories frequently have a high probability of increasing in severity by the next survey, so that escalation and repeat bleaching become common once a reef enters this range. Reefs beginning in the highest categories more often decrease in the following event, and extreme states are rarely sustained at the same level. We interpret this as severe bleaching is followed by mortality (e.g. xxx), leaving a community dominated by more tolerant taxa that can bleach in successive events (ref). These apparent declines therefore likely represent a state change driven by mass mortality rather than genuine recovery. Critically, the probability of further increase from a given starting state has itself risen across more recent intervals, indicating that the buffer between events is shrinking.

Expressing the fitted model as the probability of moderate-or-worse bleaching against thermal dose makes the change in response explicit (Fig. 4c). The event-specific exceedance curves shift progressively leftward through the record: where the 1998 and 2002 events required substantial DHW to reach a given bleaching probability, the recent events reach the same probability at far lower thermal stress, and the curves for 2016 onward sit well to the left of the early baseline. In simple terms, for an equivalent level of DHW, severe bleaching has become more likely over time.

Because the reefs surveyed in every event differ in their average thermal exposure, this  shift could in theory reflect the underlying spatial pattern rather than a change in the reefs over time. Decomposing DHW into a between-reef component (each reef's mean exposure) and a within-reef component (the deviation of each event from that mean) isolates the change occurring within individual reefs at a common chronic exposure (Fig. 4d). Even after this correction, the event curves retain their temporal ordering: at a reef experiencing its own typical DHW, the probability of moderate-or-worse bleaching rises across the record, with the recent events sitting above the early baseline. Because this contrast is identified entirely within reefs, it cannot be attributed to differences in survey composition among years, and it provides the strongest evidence that the rise in bleaching propensity reflects a genuine change in how individual reefs respond to heat.

Taken together, these results are inconsistent with an emergent, reef-wide increase in thermal tolerance on the Great Barrier Reef and give significant cause for concern. The strong re-allocation of reefs into the moderate-to-extreme categories in later years implies a system increasingly poised near its thresholds, where a smaller additional stress can compound to trigger higher categories. Responses  vary considerably among reefs in that some repeatedly escalate while others decline, consistent with thermal refugia, oceanographic buffering, or among-reef differences in community composition mediating thermal sensitivity. Given the marked interannual variability in the dose–response (Fig. 2b), future events may yet behave differently, but the GBR-wide trajectory to date offers no support for tolerance keeping pace with warming.

```{r}
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| fig-height: 9
#| fig-width: 12


panel_sf <- GBRMP_aerial_centroids |>
  dplyr::filter(as.character(year) != "2025") |>
  dplyr::group_by(LABEL_ID) |>
  dplyr::filter(dplyr::n_distinct(year) >= 7)  |>
  mutate(year = droplevels.factor(year))

panel_coords <- sf::st_coordinates(panel_sf)
A_panel  <- inla.spde.make.A(mesh = mesh, loc = panel_coords)
s_index  <- inla.spde.make.index("spatial.field", n.spde = spde$n.spde)

stack_subset <- inla.stack(
  data = list(aerial = panel_sf$aerial),
  A    = list(A_panel, 1),
  effects = list(
    c(s_index, list(Intercept = 1)),
    list(dhw  = panel_sf$dhw,
         year     = panel_sf$year,
         LABEL_ID = panel_sf$LABEL_ID)),
  tag = "panel")

inla_formula_fe <- aerial ~ dhw * year + f(LABEL_ID, model = "iid")

fm_subset <- inla(
  inla_formula_fe,
  data   = inla.stack.data(stack_subset),
  family = "pom",
  control.family    = list(control.pom = list(cdf = "logit")),
  control.predictor = list(A = inla.stack.A(stack_subset),
                           compute = TRUE, link = 1),
  control.fixed = list(
    mean.intercept = -2, prec.intercept = 1,
    mean = list(dhw_dev = 1, default = 0),   # slope centred at 1 like fm1
    prec = list(default = 0.001)),           # weak on everything else
  control.compute = list(dic = TRUE, waic = TRUE, config = TRUE,
                         return.marginals.predictor = TRUE),
  control.inla = list(control.vb = list(emergency = 30)),
  silent = 2L)



library(tidyverse)

# --- cut-points from the POM thetas (4 categories -> 3 cut-points) ---
th  <- fm_subset$summary.hyperpar[paste0("theta", 1:3, " for POM"), "mean"]
cut <- cumsum(c(th[1], exp(th[2]), exp(th[3])))   # alpha1, alpha2, alpha3
a2  <- cut[3]                                      # boundary cat2|cat3 = "theta2"

fx <- setNames(fm_subset$summary.fixed[, "mean"],
               rownames(fm_subset$summary.fixed))

# --- fixed effects ---
b0 <- fx[["(Intercept)"]]
bd <- fx[["dhw"]]
fx_mean <- fm_subset$summary.fixed[, "mean"]

years <- c(2002, 2016, 2017, 2020, 2022, 2024)    # 1998 = reference
dhw_seq <- seq(0, 16, length.out = 200)

# linear predictor per year, then P(Y >= 3) = 1 - plogis(a2 - eta)
exceed <- map_dfr(c(1998, years), function(yr) {
  yint  <- if (yr == 1998) 0 else fx[[paste0("year", yr)]]
  yslp  <- if (yr == 1998) 0 else fx[[paste0("dhw:year", yr)]]
  eta   <- b0 + dhw_seq * (bd + yslp) + yint
  tibble(year = yr, dhw = dhw_seq,
         p = 1 - plogis(a2 - eta))               # P(Y >= 3)
})


a2 <- cut[3]   # P(Y >= 3) = "moderate or worse"; use cut[3] for ">= high"

# --- observed DHW range per year (from the SAME panel the model was fit on) ---
dhw_range <- panel_sf |>
  sf::st_drop_geometry() |>
  dplyr::mutate(year = as.integer(as.character(year))) |>
  dplyr::group_by(year) |>
  dplyr::summarise(lo = min(dhw, na.rm = TRUE),
                   hi = max(dhw, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop")

years <- c(2002, 2016, 2017, 2020, 2022, 2024)
dhw_seq <- seq(0, 16, length.out = 400)

exceed_crop <- purrr::map_dfr(c(1998, years), function(yr) {
  yint <- if (yr == 1998) 0 else fx[[paste0("year", yr)]]
  yslp <- if (yr == 1998) 0 else fx[[paste0("dhw:year", yr)]]
  rng  <- dhw_range |> dplyr::filter(year == yr)
  d    <- dhw_seq[dhw_seq >= rng$lo & dhw_seq <= rng$hi]   # <-- mask to support
  eta  <- b0 + d * (bd + yslp) + yint
  tibble(year = yr, dhw = d, p = 1 - plogis(a2 - eta))
})


yr_cols <- c(`1998` = "#2c7bb6",   # baseline: cool blue, clearly "the reference"
             `2002` = "#7fb8c9",
             `2016` = "#f4a582",
             `2017` = "#e88a3c",
             `2020` = "#d6604d",
             `2022` = "#b2182b",
             `2024` = "#67001f")


standardreef <- ggplot() +
  geom_line(data=exceed, aes(dhw, p, colour = factor(year)), linewidth = 0.9) +
#  geom_line(data=exceed_crop, aes(dhw, p, colour = factor(year)), linewidth = 0.9) +
  scale_colour_manual(values=yr_cols, name = "Event") +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, 1)) +
  scale_x_continuous(limits = c(0, 16), breaks = seq(0, 16, 4)) +
  labs(x = "DHW", y = "P(bleaching ≥ moderate)",
       subtitle = "P(>60%) vs DHW") +
  theme_bw()







# --- add within/between split to the balanced-7 panel ---
panel_sf <- panel_sf |>
  dplyr::group_by(LABEL_ID) |>
  dplyr::mutate(dhw_bar = mean(dhw, na.rm = TRUE),
                dhw_dev = dhw - dhw_bar) |>
  dplyr::ungroup()

stack_subset <- inla.stack(
  data = list(aerial = panel_sf$aerial),
  A    = list(A_panel, 1),
  effects = list(
    c(s_index, list(Intercept = 1)),
    list(dhw_dev = panel_sf$dhw_dev, dhw_bar = panel_sf$dhw_bar,
         year = panel_sf$year, LABEL_ID = panel_sf$LABEL_ID)),
  tag = "panel")

inla_formula_mund <- aerial ~ dhw_dev * year + dhw_bar + f(LABEL_ID, model = "iid")

fm_mund <- inla(
  inla_formula_mund,
  data   = inla.stack.data(stack_subset),
  family = "pom",
  control.family = list(control.pom = list(cdf = "logit")),
  control.predictor = list(A = inla.stack.A(stack_subset), compute = TRUE, link = 1),
  control.fixed = list(mean.intercept = -2, prec.intercept = 1,
                       mean = list(dhw_dev = 1, default = 0),
                       prec = list(default = 0.001)),
  control.compute = list(dic = TRUE, waic = TRUE, config = TRUE),
  control.inla = list(control.vb = list(emergency = 30)),
  silent = 2L)

# --- exceedance at fixed dhw_bar = panel mean, sweeping within-reef deviation ---
th  <- fm_mund$summary.hyperpar[paste0("theta",1:3," for POM"),"mean"]
cut <- cumsum(c(th[1], exp(th[2]), exp(th[3])))
a2  <- cut[3]                                   # P(Y>=3); cut[3] for >=high
fx  <- setNames(fm_mund$summary.fixed[,"mean"], rownames(fm_mund$summary.fixed))

bar0 <- mean(panel_sf$dhw_bar)                  # fix chronic exposure at panel mean
dev_seq <- seq(-8, 8, length.out = 400)         # within-reef deviation axis

# per-year observed dev range, to mask
dev_range <- panel_sf |> sf::st_drop_geometry() |>
  dplyr::mutate(year = as.integer(as.character(year))) |>
  dplyr::group_by(year) |>
  dplyr::summarise(lo = min(dhw_dev), hi = max(dhw_dev), .groups="drop")

exceed_m <- purrr::map_dfr(c(1998, years), function(yr){
  yint <- if (yr==1998) 0 else fx[[paste0("year",yr)]]
  yslp <- if (yr==1998) 0 else fx[[paste0("dhw_dev:year",yr)]]
  eta  <- fx[["(Intercept)"]] + dev_seq*(fx[["dhw_dev"]]+yslp) + yint + fx[["dhw_bar"]]*bar0
  tibble(year=yr, dhw_dev=dev_seq, p = 1 - plogis(a2 - eta))
})

withreef <- ggplot(exceed_m, aes(dhw_dev, p, colour=factor(year))) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0, colour="grey70") +
  geom_line(linewidth=1) +
  scale_colour_manual(values=yr_cols, name="Event") +
  scale_y_continuous(limits=c(0,1)) +
  labs(x="DHW deviation from reef mean", y="P(bleaching ≥ moderate)",
       subtitle="P(>60%) at mean exposure") +
  theme_bw()




```

#### Figure 4

```{r}
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| cache: false
#| fig-height: 7
#| fig-width: 12
#| fig-cap: |
#|   **Figure 4 | Temporal change in reef bleaching state across eight events
#|   (1998–2024).**
#|   (**a**) Alluvial diagram tracking individual reefs through their bleaching
#|   category at each successive event for reefs surveyed repeatedly across all
#|   events (n = 560 surveys, 80 reefs). Bars show the number of reefs in each
#|   ordered bleaching category per event (0 = none through 5 = most severe);
#|   ribbons trace transitions of individual reefs between consecutive events.
#|   (**b**) Transition probabilities between consecutive events, conditional on a
#|   reef's bleaching category at the start of each interval (panels 1–5 = starting
#|   category): given a reef's state at the start of an interval, the probability
#|   that its bleaching had increased (dark red), stayed the same (orange) or
#|   decreased (blue) by the next survey. Bars are ordered by survey-interval start
#|   year. The probability of further increase from a given state has risen across
#|   recent intervals, indicating eroding recovery between events. The 2025 event
#|   is excluded from both panels owing to its spatially restricted survey
#|   footprint; see Fig. S9a,b for the equivalent analysis on the full dataset. 
#|   (c) Fitted probability of moderate-or-worse bleaching, P(bleaching ≥ moderate), 
#|   as a function of absolute thermal stress (DHW, °C-weeks) for each event, derived 
#|   from the proportional-odds model. Curves shift progressively leftward and 
#|   steepen across the record, so that recent events reach a given bleaching 
#|   probability at markedly lower thermal stress than the earliest events: at 
#|   any given DHW, the probability of moderate-or-worse bleaching is higher in 
#|   later events, with 2016 onward lying well above the 1998–2002 baseline. 
#|   Curves are drawn across the observed DHW range. (d) The same probability 
#|   expressed against each reef's DHW deviation from its own long-term mean (a 
#|   within-reef, Mundlak decomposition), holding chronic exposure constant so 
#|   that the curves isolate change occurring within individual reefs rather 
#|   than differences in which reefs were surveyed. The vertical line marks zero 
#|   deviation (a reef experiencing its typical thermal stress). The event curves 
#|   retain their temporal ordering after this correction, with recent events 
#|   sitting above the earlier baseline at equivalent within-reef exposure, 
#|   indicating that the rise in bleaching probability reflects a genuine change 
#|   in how individual reefs respond to heat and is not an artefact of survey 
#|   composition among years.

library(patchwork)
library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(ggalluvial)
library(sf)

## load functions

plot_alluvial <- function(path,
                           year_min = -Inf,
                           year_max = Inf) {
 

interpcolors_aerial_score_full <- c("#ABD9E9", "#FFFFBF", "#FEE090", "#FDAE61", "#F46D43", "#D73027")

  df <- readRDS(path) |>
    st_make_valid() |>
    as.data.frame() |>
    filter(year > year_min, year < year_max)

  ids_all_years <- df |>
    distinct(LABEL_ID, year) |>
    count(LABEL_ID, name = "nyears") |>
    filter(nyears == n_distinct(df$year)) |>
    pull(LABEL_ID)

  GBR_ID_reefs <- df |>
    filter(LABEL_ID %in% ids_all_years) |>
    select(LABEL_ID, year, max_aerial_score)

  formatted_data <- GBR_ID_reefs |>
    rename(subject = LABEL_ID, response = max_aerial_score, survey = year) |>
    group_by(survey, subject, response) |>
    summarise(freq = n(), .groups = "drop") |>
    mutate(
      survey   = factor(survey, levels = sort(unique(df$year))),
      response = factor(as.character(response), levels = as.character(0:5))
    )

  ggplot(
    formatted_data,
    aes(
      x = survey,
      stratum = response,
      alluvium = subject,
      y = freq,
      fill = response,
      label = response
    )
  ) +
    geom_flow(color = "black", fill = "lightgrey") +
    geom_stratum(alpha = 0.8) +
    geom_text(stat = "stratum", size = 3) +
    scale_fill_manual(values = interpcolors_aerial_score_full) +
    theme_bw() +
    theme(legend.position = "none") +
    labs(x = "", y = expression(italic(n)~"reefs"))
}

plot_transitions <- function(path,
                       year_min = -Inf,
                       year_max = Inf,
                       bleached_only = TRUE){

  df_transitions <- readRDS(path) |>
    st_make_valid() |>
    as.data.frame() |>
    filter(year > year_min, year < year_max)

  trans_bleached <- df_transitions |>
    transmute(
      reef_id = LABEL_ID,
      year    = year,
      state   = max_aerial_score
    ) |>
    arrange(reef_id, year) |>
    group_by(reef_id) |>
    mutate(
      state_next = lead(state),
      year_next  = lead(year)
    ) |>
    ungroup() |>
    filter(!is.na(state_next))

  if (bleached_only) {
    trans_bleached <- trans_bleached |>
      filter(state > 0)
  }

  trans_bleached <- trans_bleached |>
    mutate(
      direction = case_when(
        state_next > state ~ "increase",
        state_next < state ~ "decrease",
        TRUE               ~ "same"
      )
    )


  year_map <- trans_bleached |>
    group_by(year) |>
    summarise(year_next = min(year_next, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") |>
    arrange(year) |>
    mutate(label = paste0(year, " - ", year_next)) |>
    dplyr::pull(label, year)

  p_bleached_by_year_state <- trans_bleached |>
    count(year, state, direction, name = "n") |>
    group_by(year, state) |>
    mutate(probability = n / sum(n)) |>
    ungroup() |>
    mutate(
      direction = factor(direction, levels = rev(c("decrease", "same", "increase"))),
      year  = factor(year, levels = names(year_map)),
      state = factor(state)
    )

  
  pal <- c(
  "#009E73",
  "#61B879",
  "#B5CD8A",
  "#FFF7BC",
  "#FDBF6F",
  "#F97B3D",
  "#E31A1C"

)

pal



  ggplot(p_bleached_by_year_state) +
    geom_col(aes(year, probability, fill = direction), color = "black") +
    facet_wrap(~ state, nrow = 1, drop = FALSE) +
    theme_bw() +
    scale_fill_manual(values = c("#F97B3D", "#FFF7BC", "#B5CD8A")) +
    scale_x_discrete(labels = year_map) +
    labs(
      x = "Survey interval start year",
      y = "Transition probability"
    ) +
    theme(
      axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 90, vjust = 0.5, hjust = 1)
    )
}



fig3a <- plot_alluvial(
  path = "/Users/rof011/GBR-dhw/datasets/gbr_shape_aerial_OISST.rds",
  year_min = -Inf,
  year_max = 2026
)


fig3b <- plot_transitions(
    path = "/Users/rof011/GBR-dhw/datasets/gbr_shape_aerial_OISST.rds",
    year_min = -Inf,
    year_max = 2026
  ) +
  theme(
  legend.position = c(0.94, 0.67),
  legend.justification = c(1, 0),
  legend.background = element_rect(fill = "white", colour = "transparent"),
  legend.box.background = element_rect(fill = "white", colour = "transparent"),
  legend.text = element_text(size = rel(0.8)),
  legend.title = element_blank(),
  legend.key.size = unit(0.8, "lines")
)

leftside  <- fig3a / fig3b
rightside <- standardreef / withreef

figure3 <- (leftside | rightside) +
  plot_layout(widths = c(1, 0.3)) +
  plot_annotation(tag_levels = "A", tag_prefix = "(", tag_suffix = ")")

figure3

#ggsave(figure3, filename="~/Desktop/figure3.pdf", dpi="retina", width=12, height=7, units="in")


```

### The future of the Great Barrier Reef in the Anthropocene

- The data show no evidence of adaptive thermal tolerance keeping pace with warming on the GBR, while models indicate contextual vulnerability is rising, not falling, which removes a key assumption underpinning optimistic recovery projections.
- Severe bleaching now occurs at thermal doses that produced only mild bleaching two decades ago, meaning existing DHW-based management thresholds (e.g. bleaching watch/warning tiers) may understate biological risk and warrant recalibration.
- The shrinking recovery interval between events implies that management strategies premised on reefs recovering between disturbances are increasingly unsafe; intervention timing should assume compounding rather than independent events.
- Rising contextual vulnerability means emissions trajectories, not local management alone, set the ceiling on reef futures: local interventions can buy time but cannot offset a systemic upward shift in the dose–response.

- Restoration and intervention (assisted gene flow, larval seeding, shading) should be evaluated against a moving baseline of rising vulnerability, not a static one; success metrics calibrated to historical conditions risk overstating gains.
- Among-reef heterogeneity in response points to refugia identification and protection as a defensible near-term priority, while acknowledging refugia themselves are a shrinking and time-limited asset.
- Monitoring investment should preserve and extend long repeat-survey time series, since the within-reef signal (not one-off spatial snapshots) distinguishes genuine vulnerability change from sampling artefact.

- The loss of the Great Barrier Reef is unlikely to arrive as a single catastrophic event. Reefs differ markedly in their response, and those with high larval supply or in thermal refugia can rebound strongly after individual bleaching events, sustaining a patchwork of recovery across the system.
- The greater risk is cumulative. As events recur on shortening intervals, a growing fraction of reefs fail to fully recover between disturbances, and this incomplete recovery compounds: degraded reefs seed fewer larvae and re-bleach from a lower baseline, eroding the capacity of the wider system to recover from the events that follow. The trajectory is therefore better understood as a ratchet than a cliff, with each event lowering the ceiling for the next.

- Against this backdrop, the policy response remains misaligned with the scale of the driver. Local interventions such as restoration and water-quality improvement, although well funded, act on stressors that are secondary to temperature and cannot offset a systemic upward shift in bleaching vulnerability driven by warming. Meanwhile, 11 new coal mine projects have been approved in Queensland since the onset of recurrent mass bleaching in 2016, representing approximately 121 Mt CO₂-e  of direct lifetime emissions, resulting in at least ~1.8 Gt CO₂-e of lifetime coal-use emissions.
- The central finding of this study, that reefs are becoming more likely to bleach at any given level of heat, underscores that the future of the reef will be determined principally by the rate of global decarbonisation, and that mitigation at the scale of the driver cannot be substituted by intervention at the scale of the symptom.
